revision.text:
'''日延'''（にちえん、生没年不詳）は、[[平安時代]]中期の[[天台宗]]の僧。[[肥前国]]の出身。権律師[[仁観]]の弟子。
中国天台山から[[延暦寺]]に対し中国で散逸した天台教籍の書写送付の依頼があり、[[953年]]（天暦7年）その送使として[[呉越国]]へ渡った。呉越王から紫衣が贈られ、[[953年]]（天徳元年）日本に伝わっていない新暦法の[[符天暦]]と内典・外典約1000巻を携えて帰国した。[[村上天皇]]はその功を賞して[[僧綱]][[宣旨]]を贈ろうとしたが、日延はそれを辞退して九州の[[大宰府]]に下り、[[康保]]年間（[[964年]]－[[968年]]）[[藤原師輔]]のために[[大浦寺]]を建立した。符天暦の請来は日本における宿曜道成立のもととなった。
{{stub}} [[Category:僧 (日本)|にちえん]] [[Category:天台系仏教|にちえん]] [[Category:平安時代の人物|にちえん]]

'''日延'''（にちえん、生没年不詳）は、[[平安時代]]中期の[[天台宗]]の僧。[[肥前国]]の出身。権律師[[仁観]]の弟子。
中国天台山から[[延暦寺]]に対し中国で散逸した天台教籍の書写送付の依頼があり、[[953年]]（[[天暦]]7年）その送使として[[呉越|呉越国]]へ渡った。呉越王から紫衣が贈られ、[[953年]]（[[天徳]]元年）日本に伝わっていない新暦法の[[符天暦]]と内典・外典約1000巻を携えて帰国した。[[村上天皇]]はその功を賞して[[僧綱]][[宣旨]]を贈ろうとしたが、日延はそれを辞退して九州の[[大宰府]]に下り、[[康保]]年間（[[964年]]－[[968年]]）[[藤原師輔]]のために[[大浦寺]]を建立した。符天暦の請来は日本における宿曜道成立のもととなった。
{{stub}} [[Category:僧 (日本)|にちえん]] [[Category:天台系仏教|にちえん]] [[Category:平安時代の人物|にちえん]]

revision.text:
'''日延'''（にちえん、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[平安時代]]中期の[[天台宗]]の僧。[[肥前国]]の出身。権律師[[仁観]]の弟子。
中国天台山から[[延暦寺]]に対し中国で散逸した天台教籍の書写送付の依頼があり、[[953年]]（[[天暦]]7年）その送使として[[呉越|呉越国]]へ渡った。呉越王[[銭俶]]から紫衣が贈られて[[司天台]]（天文台）で学ぶ特別な許可を与えられ、[[957年]]（[[天徳 (日本)|天徳]]元年）日本に伝わっていない新暦法の[[符天暦]]と内典・外典約1000巻を携えて帰国した。[[村上天皇]]はその功を賞して[[僧綱]][[宣旨]]を贈ろうとしたが、日延はそれを辞退して九州の[[大宰府]]に下り、[[康保]]年間（[[964年]] - [[968年]]）[[藤原師輔]]のために[[大浦寺]]を建立した。符天暦の請来は日本における[[宿曜道]]成立のもととなった。ただし、『[[二中歴]]』には、日延は宿曜師ではなく、人の骨相や生年月日から人の運命を占う[[禄命]]に通じた[[禄命師]]として扱われており、宿曜道よりも禄命の分野における活動が知られていたようである。
{{DEFAULTSORT:にちえん}} [[Category:日本の僧]] [[Category:天台宗の僧]] [[Category:平安時代の人物]] [[Category:肥前国の人物]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:没年不明]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

{{Otheruses|{{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}}, a monk of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}|Other uses of {{ill|Nichien|ja|日延}}|日延 (曖昧さ回避)}}
'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. He was a native of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師}}.
At the request of {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国}} in {{ill|953|ja|953年}} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|ja|957年}} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}} intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} in Kyushu. During the {{ill|Kōhō era|ja|康保}} ({{ill|964|ja|964年}}–{{ill|968|ja|968年}}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}
[[Category:Monks of the Heian period]] [[Category:Tendai monks]] [[Category:People from Hizen Province]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Sukuyōdō]] [[ja:日延]]

'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. He was a native of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師}}.
At the request of {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国}} in {{ill|953|ja|953年}} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|ja|957年}} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}} intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} in Kyushu. During the {{ill|Kōhō era|ja|康保}} ({{ill|964|ja|964年}}–{{ill|968|ja|968年}}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

At the request of {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国}} in {{ill|953|ja|953rd year }} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7th year }}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|ja|957th year }} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor {{ill|Murakami|ja|村上天皇}} intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to {{ill|Dazaifu|ja|大宰府}} in Kyushu. During the {{ill|Kōhō era|ja|康保}} ({{ill|964|ja|964th year }}–{{ill|968|ja|968th year }}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

{{distinguish|Nichiren}}
'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monk from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. He was a native of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}} and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師}}.
At the request of {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国}} in {{ill|953|ja|953rd year }} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7th year }}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|ja|957th year }} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.

During the {{ill|Kōhō era|ja|康保}} ({{ill|964|ja|964th year }}–{{ill|968|ja|968th year }}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. {{distinguish|Nichiren}}

'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. He was a native of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.

At the request of [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Mount Tiantai]] in China to [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in [[:en:953|953]] ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.

Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. During the [[:en:Kōhō|Kōhō era]] ([[:en:964|964]]–[[:en:968|968]]), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].

Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|qq=|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.

At the request of [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Mount Tiantai]] in China to [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in [[:en:953|953]] ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.

Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. During the [[:en:Kōhō|Kōhō era]] ([[:en:964|964]]–[[:en:968|968]]), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].

Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|qq=|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.

In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.

Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. During the [[:en:Kōhō|Kōhō era]] ([[:en:964|964]]–[[:en:968|968]]), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].

Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.

There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.

Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. During the [[:en:Kōhō|Kōhō era]] ([[:en:964|964]]–[[:en:968|968]]), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].

Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.

He was a native of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.

There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.

Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. '''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. He was a native of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.
At the request of [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Mount Tiantai]] in China to [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in [[:en:953|953]] ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. During the [[:en:Kōhō|Kōhō era]] ([[:en:964|964]]–[[:en:968|968]]), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monk from the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. He was a native of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]] and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.
At the request of [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Mount Tiantai]] in China to [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in [[:en:953|953]] ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In [[:en:957|957]] ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Murakami]] intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] in Kyushu. During the [[:en:Kōhō|Kōhō era]] ([[:en:964|964]]–[[:en:968|968]]), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.

'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} monk from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. He was a native of {{ill|Hizen Province|en|Hizen Province|WD=Q906926|lt=Hizen Province}} and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.
At the request of {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|en|Tiantai Mountain|WD=Q1146118|lt=Mount Tiantai}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in {{ill|953|en|953|WD=Q23586|lt=953}} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|en|957|WD=Q23711|lt=957}} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami}} intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} in Kyushu. During the {{ill|Kōhō|en|Kōhō|WD=Q1327182|lt=Kōhō era}} ({{ill|964|en|964|WD=Q23951|lt=964}}–{{ill|968|en|968|WD=Q23918|lt=968}}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|en|Fujiwara no Morosuke|WD=Q325207|lt=Fujiwara no Morosuke}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai}} monk from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. He was a native of {{ill|Hizen Province|en|Hizen Province|WD=Q906926|lt=Hizen Province}} and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.
At the request of {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|en|Tiantai Mountain|WD=Q1146118|lt=Mount Tiantai}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in {{ill|953|en|953|WD=Q23586|lt=953}} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|en|957|WD=Q23711|lt=957}} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami}} intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} in Kyushu. During the {{ill|Kōhō|en|Kōhō|WD=Q1327182|lt=Kōhō era}} ({{ill|964|en|964|WD=Q23951|lt=964}}–{{ill|968|en|968|WD=Q23918|lt=968}}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|en|Fujiwara no Morosuke|WD=Q325207|lt=Fujiwara no Morosuke}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.

During the {{ill|Kōhō|en|Kōhō|WD=Q1327182|lt=Kōhō era}} ({{ill|964|en|964|WD=Q23951|lt=964}}–{{ill|968|en|968|WD=Q23918|lt=968}}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|en|Fujiwara no Morosuke|WD=Q325207|lt=Fujiwara no Morosuke}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Nijūreki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日延]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日延]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日延]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}|occupation={{ill|bhikkhu|WD=Q854997|1=Bhikkhu}}}} {{distinguish|Nichiren}}
'''Nichien''' (日延, dates of birth and death unknown) was a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|WD=Q668188|lt=Tendai|1=Tiantai}} monk from the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. He was a native of {{ill|Hizen Province|en|Hizen Province|WD=Q906926|lt=Hizen Province|1=Hizen Province}} and a disciple of {{ill|Ninkan|ja|仁観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who held the position of {{ill|Gon-risshi|ja|権律師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gon-risshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧綱|1=Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7667596}}.
At the request of {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|en|Tiantai Mountain|WD=Q1146118|lt=Mount Tiantai|1=Tiantai Mountain}} in China to {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}, seeking copies of Tendai texts that had been scattered in China, Nichien was dispatched to {{ill|Wuyue|ja|呉越国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuyue|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 呉越|1=Wuyue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1193822}} in {{ill|953|en|953|WD=Q23586|lt=953|1=953}} ({{ill|Tenryaku 7|ja|天暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as an envoy. There, he was granted a purple robe by King {{ill|Qian Chu|ja|銭俶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qian Chu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 銭弘俶|1=Qian Chu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1036789}} of Wuyue and received special permission to study at the {{ill|Si Tiantai|ja|司天台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Si Tiantai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (astronomical observatory). In {{ill|957|en|957|WD=Q23711|lt=957|1=957}} ({{ill|Tentoku 1|ja|天徳元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tentoku 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he returned to Japan bringing with him the {{ill|Futen Calendar|ja|符天暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futen Calendar|qq=}}, a new calendar system not yet known in Japan, along with approximately 1,000 volumes of Buddhist and non-Buddhist texts.
Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} intended to award Nichien a {{ill|sōgō senji|ja|僧綱宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sōgō senji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (imperial appointment for monks) in recognition of his achievements, but Nichien declined the honor and instead went to {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}} in Kyushu. During the {{ill|Kōhō|en|Kōhō|WD=Q1327182|lt=Kōhō era|1=Kōhō}} ({{ill|964|en|964|WD=Q23951|lt=964|1=964}}–{{ill|968|en|968|WD=Q23918|lt=968|1=968}}), he established {{ill|Ōura-ji|ja|大浦寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōura-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|en|Fujiwara no Morosuke|WD=Q325207|lt=Fujiwara no Morosuke|1=Fujiwara no Morosuke}}.
Nichien's introduction of the Futen Calendar laid the foundation for the development of {{ill|Sukuyōdō|ja|宿曜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukuyōdō|qq=}} (astrology) in Japan. However, according to the {{ill|Nijūreki|ja|二中歴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūreki|1=Q11371277|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11371277}}, Nichien was not regarded as a Sukuyō master but rather as a {{ill|Rokumeishi|ja|禄命師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumeishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an expert in {{ill|Rokumei|ja|禄命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokumei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—a form of divination that determines a person's fate based on bone structure and birth date. It appears his activities were more prominently associated with Rokumei than with Sukuyōdō.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}
